coredata.dc:title: High volumetric-energy-density flexible supercapacitors based on PEDOT:PSS incorporated with carbon quantum dots hybrid electrodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Dinh Cung Tien
Kim, Seonghan
Kim, Bo-Seok
Kim, Sejung
Lee, Soo-Hyoung
coredata.dc:description: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) is a highly successful conductive polymer utilized as an electrode material in energy storage units for portable and wearable electronic devices. Nevertheless, employing PEDOT:PSS in supercapacitors (SC) in its pristine state presents challenges due to its suboptimal electrochemical performance and operational instability. To surmount these limitations, PEDOT:PSS has been integrated with carbon-based materials to form flexible electrodes, which exhibit physical and chemical stability during SC operation. We developed a streamlined fabrication process for high-performance SC electrodes composed of PEDOT:PSS and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs were synthesized under microwave irradiation, yielding green- and red-light emissions. Through optimizing the ratios of CQDs to PEDOT:PSS, the SC electrodes were prepared using a spray-coating technique, marking a significant improvement in device performance with a high volumetric capacitance (104.10 F cm−3), impressive energy density (19.68 Wh cm−3), and excellent cyclic stability, retaining ∼85 % of its original volumetric capacitance after 15,000 repeated GCD cycles. Moreover, the SCs, when utilized as a flexible substrate, demonstrated the ability to maintain up to ∼85 % of their electrochemical performance even after 3,000 bending cycles (at a bending angle of 60°). These attributes render this hybrid composite an ideal candidate for a lightweight smart energy storage component in portable and wearable electronic technologies.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEDOT:PSS
Carbon quantum dots
Hybrid electrode
Supercapacitor
Flexible power sources

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the pH-GOx, pH-Gy, and pH-Ry

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) through a grant provided by the Korean government (No. NRF- 2021R1F1A1063451 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram for producing G- and R-CQDs under microwave irradiation.
Elemental analysis of G-CQD and R-CQD by using X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) of (a) C 1s, (b) O 1s, and (c) N 1s. Size distribution of (d) G-CQD and (e) R-CQD.
(a) High-resolution XPS spectra and (b) peak shifts of the S 2p of pH, pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05.
Typical SEM images and EDS elemental mapping images of O, C, S, and N of (a) pH, (b) pH-GO1, (c) pH-R0.05, and (d) pH-G0.05 thin film electrodes.

Electrical performance of the SC utilizing pH, pH-GO1, pH-R0.05, and pH-G0.05 electrodes includes: (a) CV curves at 10 mV s–1, (b) GCD profiles at a specified current of 0.14 A cm–3, (c) volumetric capacitance across various specific currents from 0.14 to 2.78 A cm–3, (d) Ragone plots, (e) Nyquist plots recorded over frequencies from 106 to 10−2 Hz with an AC perturbation amplitude of 5 mV. An inset illustrates the corresponding equivalent circuit. (f) Demonstrates the long cycle life over 500 GCD cycles at a constant current density of 2.78 A cm–3.

(a) The extended cycle life over 15,000 GCD cycles of a pH-G0.05 electrode-based SC at a consistent current density of 2.78 A cm–3. The inset presents the GCD curves for the first (dark gray), 1,000th (red), 5,000th (blue), 10,000th (green), and 15,000th (violet) cycles. (b) Features a Ragone plot assessing volumetric energy and power densities (W cm–3) relative to previously documented values [1,29–36].

The mechanical bending durability of the flexible SC with pH-G0.05 electrodes is examined. (a) Depicts photographs of the SC in both flat and bent configurations. The SC device's electrochemical properties at various bending angles are detailed, including (b) CV curves at a 50 mV s–1 scan rate, (c) GCD profiles at a current density of 0.17 A cm–3, and (d) capacitance retention concerning the number of bending angles. The electrochemical performance of the SC device under a uniform bending cycle at a set bending angle (60°) is captured through (e) CV curves at a 50 mV s–1 scan rate, (f) GCD profiles at a 0.17 A cm–3 current density, and (g) capacitance retention relating to the number of bending cycles.

head.ce:title: High volumetric-energy-density flexible supercapacitors based on PEDOT:PSS incorporated with carbon quantum dots hybrid electrodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) is a highly successful conductive polymer utilized as an electrode material in energy storage units for portable and wearable electronic devices. Nevertheless, employing PEDOT:PSS in supercapacitors (SC) in its pristine state presents challenges due to its suboptimal electrochemical performance and operational instability. To surmount these limitations, PEDOT:PSS has been integrated with carbon-based materials to form flexible electrodes, which exhibit physical and chemical stability during SC operation. We developed a streamlined fabrication process for high-performance SC electrodes composed of PEDOT:PSS and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The CQDs were synthesized under microwave irradiation, yielding green- and red-light emissions. Through optimizing the ratios of CQDs to PEDOT:PSS, the SC electrodes were prepared using a spray-coating technique, marking a significant improvement in device performance with a high volumetric capacitance (104.10 F cm−3), impressive energy density (19.68 Wh cm−3), and excellent cyclic stability, retaining ∼85 % of its original volumetric capacitance after 15,000 repeated GCD cycles. Moreover, the SCs, when utilized as a flexible substrate, demonstrated the ability to maintain up to ∼85 % of their electrochemical performance even after 3,000 bending cycles (at a bending angle of 60°). These attributes render this hybrid composite an ideal candidate for a lightweight smart energy storage component in portable and wearable electronic technologies.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapidly growing demands for portable and wearable electronic technologies have spurred essential research into miniaturized, lightweight, and mechanically adaptable energy storage devices [1–5]. Supercapacitors (SC) stand out by offering superior performances over lithium-ion batteries and electrolytic capacitors, characterized by excellent power density (> 1 kW kg–1), robust cycling stability, broad operating temperature ranges, swift charge/discharge times, high flexibility, compact volume, and safe operation [6–8]. For crafting high-performance SCs suitable as flexible power sources, the development of thin-film SCs, which combine exceptional mechanical flexibility with superior energy storage capabilities, has been identified as a crucial consideration [9,10].

To boost the electrochemical performances of SCs, such as power and energy density, significant progress in electrode materials is imperative [7,10]. Carbon-based materials (e.g., activated carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene), transition metal oxides (e.g., RuO2, MnO2, CoO x , NiO, and Fe2O3), and conductive polymers (e.g., polypyrrole-PPy, polyaniline-PANI, poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):polystyrenesulfonate-PEDOT:PSS) have been explored as promising electrode materials for SCs [11–15]. Of these, PEDOT:PSS is particularly notable as the most commercially prevalent active material, with its exceptional processability for thin film formation attributable to favorable water compatibility, excellent thermal stability, remarkable flexibility, and outstanding elasticity [1,16]. However, despite its pseudocapacitive nature, pristine PEDOT:PSS falls short in long-term cycling stability owing to its moderate electrical conductivity and susceptibility to structural damage during redox processes [16,17]. Consequently, to ameliorate these aspects, the exploration of hybrid materials comprising PEDOT:PSS and secondary dopants for multifunctional electrode materials has gained momentum, aiming to enhance heterogeneous electrical properties, capacitor behaviors, and cycle life [17].

Vilaseca's group [18] demonstrated the augmentation of PEDOT:PSS with PPy to construct cellulose nanopapers-based SCs that boast improved electrical conductivity and thermal stability, arising from the synergistic interaction between PEDOT:PSS and PPy. Nevertheless, these SCs still call for optimization in terms of device capacitance and cyclic stability. Additionally, the integration of PEDOT:PSS with transition metal oxide/sulfide and two-dimensional materials encompassing transition metal carbides/nitrides/carbonitrides (MXenes) as secondary dopants have emerged as a strategic approach to engender SC devices characterized by heightened conductivity, superior capacitance, and elevated energy/power densities [19–21]. However, these doping materials are high cost, high toxicity, and potential for environmental contamination, as well as requiring complex synthesis processes [22]. Additionally, the poor cyclic stability of such SC devices remains a major challenge, significantly impeding their widespread use [23].

The incorporation of carbon-based materials, endowed with double-layer capacitance properties, into pseudocapacitive PEDOT:PSS heralds a promising avenue for the creation of hybrid active electrode materials aimed at enhancing the electrochemical performances of SCs with unparalleled long-term cyclic stability [24]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), unique for their strong quantum confinement and edge effects which confer exceptional electronic conductivity, chemical stability, vast specific surface area, and exemplary cyclic stability [25–28], stand as an ideal choice for this endeavor [23–26]. Additionally, due to their facile and rapid synthesis from a variety of readily accessible carbon sources, including natural and biomass materials [27], CQDs, adorned with functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, and –NH2, exhibit superior solution-processability over other carbon-based materials and conductive polymers like PPy and PANI [26]. These functional groups facilitate their dispersion into PEDOT:PSS aqueous dispersions, yielding a homogenous hybrid electrode material that enhances electron diffusion within the composite [25]. Incorporating CQDs into PEDOT:PSS enables modification of its inhomogeneous conductivity and intrinsic properties by reducing PSS content [29]. This enhances the formation of a robust π-π bonding network between PEDOT and CQDs, thereby improving interfacial contact area and electronic conductivity. These improvements are expected to yield flexible SCs with exceptional electrochemical energy performance, characterized by increased power/energy densities and robust charge/discharge cyclic stability [29,30].

Herein, we detail the developmental process for fabricating PEDOT:PSS electrodes for flexible SCs, incorporating readily processable CQDs as secondary dopants. The CQDs were rapidly synthesized by employing precursors structured to tune their bandgaps under microwave irradiation, completing the process in just 15 min. The construction of the hybrid SC electrodes, combining PEDOT:PSS with CQDs (pH-CQD), was realized through a low-temperature solution-processing method, evidencing an enhancement in electronic conductivity. The performance of the symmetrical, double-layer, all-solid-state SC, which utilizes an active electrode material formulated via optimized spray-coating of pH-CQD, markedly surpasses that of prior conductive polymer-based SCs and even commercially available graphene oxide (GO) [31–38]. Employing a spray-coating technique produces electrode films with open pore structures, characterized by pronounced porosity and surface roughness, thereby significantly increasing the number of interfaces between the active sites of SC electrodes and electrolytes to augment storage capacitance. Additionally, the SC, when implemented on a flexible substrate, showcased exceptional capacitance retention through 3000 bending cycles. The active electrode material forged in this study paves the way for the emergence of next-generation SCs, distinguished by their improved energy density and device stability, suitable for integration within portable, wearable, and flexible electronics.

2-propanol (IPA, C3H8O2, 99.5 %), D-sorbitol (99+%, C6H14O6), poly(vinyl alcohol) (Mw 89,000; 98 %), phosphoric acid (H3PO4, 99.999 %), citric acid (C6H8O7,
≥
99.5 %), urea (CH4N2O, 99.0–100.5 %), and diisopropylamine (
≥
99.5 %) were sourced from Sigma-Aldrich Co., USA. Acetone (C3H6O2,
≥
99.5 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 35.0 %–37.0 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, bead form) were procured from Samchun Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd., Korea. Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene)-poly(styrenesulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) was obtained from HS Advanced Materials Co., Ltd., Heraeus, Chungbuk Province, Korea. HC-graphene oxide (GO, 5 g L–1, brown, flake size ranging from 0.5 to 5 µm, and a thickness of 1 atomic layer-at least 60 %) was procured from Graphene Supermarket, Graphene Laboratories Inc, USA. ITO-coated glass substrates were purchased from the Korea Electronics Technology Institute, Korea. o -phenylenediamine was sourced from ACROS ORGANICS, USA. PTFE membrane was acquired from Advantec, Tokyo Roshi Kaisha, Ltd., Japan. Stainless-steel foil (0.1 mm thick, type 304) was procured from Alfa Aesar, Ward Hill, MA, USA.

For the synthesis of green light-emitting CQDs (G-CQDs), 0.1 g of citric acid and 0.2 g of urea were mixed in 10 mL of deionized (DI) water. This mixture underwent graphitization at 180 °C for 10 min under microwave irradiation, followed by natural cooling to ambient temperature. The resulting dark-brown solution was then purified through centrifugation and filtration using a PTFE membrane. To eliminate any unreacted precursors, this solution was subjected to dialysis with a molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of 3.5 kDa for 48 h; subsequently, G-CQD powders were collected post-freeze-drying for 48 h. For the production of red light-emitting CQDs (R-CQDs), a similar procedure was employed, with a modification in the precursor materials; here, o-phenylenediamine (0.218 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of DI water along with 200 µL of concentrated HCl; followed by a neutralization process using 2.5 mL of 1.25 M NaOH after the graphitization step. The detailed schematic of the production process is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the pH-GOx, pH-Gy, and pH-Ry
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture of IPA:Dynol (49.8:0.2 vol%) was incorporated into pristine PEDOT:PSS to adjust its hydrophilicity [39], resulting in a sample denoted as pH. For comparative analysis, various concentrations of commercial GO dispersions were integrated into the pH solution, with the resulting mixtures labeled as pH-GOx, where x represents the GO concentrations of 1, 2, 3, and 5 mg mL–1. Similarly, to prepare mixtures of pH with G-CQD and R-CQD, CQD solutions of varying concentrations were gradually added to the pH solution, resulting in samples denoted as pH-Gy and pH-Ry, where y specifies the CQD concentrations of 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, and 1 mg mL–1, respectively. The volume ratio of pH to either GO or CQD solutions was established at 3:1. Subsequently, the dispersions were agitated at 700 rpm for 1 h prior to their application in subsequent experiments.

The PVA/H3PO4 electrolyte was synthesized following our previously reported methods [39,40]. 1 g of PVA was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and subsequently heated to 90 °C under adequate stirring for 3 h. Afterward, 0.8 g of H3PO4 was gradually introduced into the mixture and stirred continuously at room temperature for 12 h to yield a homogenous gel of PVA/H3PO4 electrolyte.

Fig. S1 in Supplementary materials presents the detailed fabrication process. The freshly prepared pH, pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05 dispersions were spray-coated onto the pre-cleaned glass/ITO substrates to produce the corresponding working electrodes. Subsequently, an adequate volume of PVA/H3PO4 electrolyte was drop-casted onto the surface of the dried electrodes and left at ambient temperature for 3 h to form a gelatinous film. In the final step, two working electrodes were assembled face-to-face to create a sandwich-structured SC device. This solid-state SC features an electrode with a thickness of approximately 1.2 μm and an active area measuring 3 cm2. For the fabrication of flexible SC, a pliable stainless-steel foil was utilized in lieu of the conventional rigid glass/ITO substrate.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence measurements were conducted using a Jasco V-6∼70 UV–VIS-NIR spectrophotometer and a Jasco FP-6500 spectrofluorometer, respectively, both situated at the Future Energy Convergence Core Center (FECC) at Jeonbuk National University. The particle size distribution was characterized through transmission electron microscopy (TEM) at an operating voltage of 200 kV with an FEI Tecnai F20 TEM/STEM. High-resolution SEM (Hitachi S-4700 and S4800, Japan) was utilized for analyzing the morphology of the thin films. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), integrated with a SEM system, facilitated the acquisition of EDS elemental maps. Thin film thickness measurements were conducted using an AlphaStep®D-100 profilometer (KLA Instruments, Milpitas, CA, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were performed with a Kratos analytical probe (Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan) equipped with a monochromatic Al Kα X-ray source (hѵ = 1486.6 eV) for charge compensation. The electrical conductivity (σ, S cm–1) of thin films was determined with the 4-point probe technique, utilizing the equation σ = 1 / (ρ sheet × t), where ρ sheet represents the sheet resistance (Ω sq–1) obtained from the 4-point probe method, and t signifies the film thickness in centimeters. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), photo-charge/galvano-discharge, EIS (at frequencies from 0.1 MHz to 0.01 Hz with a 5 mV AC perturbation amplitude), and self-discharge evaluations were performed utilizing a potentiostat (ZIVE SP1, WonATech, Seoul, Korea). The specific capacitance (C, F cm–2) was derived from GCD profiles according to:
(1)
C =
(
I ×
t 1
)
/
(
V ×
S 1
)

Here, I indicates the applied current, t 1 denotes the discharging time in seconds, V represents the operational voltage window after the initial voltage drop, and S 1 corresponds to the effective area of the electrodes. The specific power (P, in W cm–2) and energy (E, in Wh cm–2) metrics were calculated with the equations:
(2)
P = E /
t 1
(3)
E = 0.5 C
( V )
2
/ 3600

To fabricate PEDOT:PSS including CQDs for SC electrodes, CQDs were first synthesized under microwave irradiation (Fig. 1). The synthesized CQDs exhibit a lattice spacing of 0.246 nm and have similar diameter ranges from 3 to 4 nm, wherein the average particle sizes of approximately 3.67 nm (G-CQDs) and approximately 4.14 nm (R-CQDs), as confirmed by TEM (Figs. 2 (d, e) and S2). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) elucidated the chemical compositions of the as-prepared CQDs through three distinctive spectra of C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s (Fig. 2(a–c)). As illustrated in Fig. 2(a), the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s were deconvoluted to reveal four binding energy peaks at approximately 284.61, 285.76, 287.17, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to C–C/CC, C–O/C–N, N–CO/CO, and –COO groups, respectively [41,42]. The O 1s spectrum reveals two discrete peaks at around 530.4 eV (CO) and around 533.4 eV (C–OH) (Fig. 2(b)) [43]. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectra of N 1s display three peaks at 398.89 eV (pyridinic N), 400.01 eV (pyrrolic N), and 400.67 eV (graphitic N) (Fig. 2(c)) [29,43,44].

Different concentrations of CQDs were incorporated into pristine PEDOT:PSS (pH) at room temperature via solution processing, resulting in a thin film thickness of approximately 1.2 µm (Fig. S3). For comparison, a mixture of PEDOT:PSS with commercial GO (pH-GOx) was also prepared. Conductivity measurements of pH-GOx, pH-Gy, and pH-Ry thin films on glass substrates were conducted utilizing a four-point probe method, in-plane, across a 1-mm channel. The integration of sufficient GO and CQDs into PEDOT:PSS (pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05) significantly enhanced electrical properties compared to the pristine pH (Fig. S4). This enhancement is attributed to the formation of π-π bonding networks between PEDOT domains and GO and/or CQDs within the PEDOT:PSS matrix, which effectively increases electrical conductivity [29]. These networks facilitate highly increased conductive pathways arising from phase separations between PEDOT and PSS, owing to the strong interactions between carbon-based materials and PEDOT chains [30], leading to reorientation of PEDOT chains and rapid carrier transport within the mixture [30]. Excessive doping material usage, however, results in particle aggregation on the film surface, reducing electrical conductivity (Fig. S4) with pH-GO2∼5, pH-G0.1∼1, and pH-R0.1∼1 samples manifesting this effect [39].

The incorporation of carbon-based materials, including GO and CQDs, with PEDOT:PSS was evidenced by high-resolution XPS spectra of S2p (Fig. 3 ). The deconvolution revealed peaks at approximately 163.59 and 164.82 eV, assigned to S 2P3/2 and S 2P1/2, respectively, which originate from the PEDOT (Fig. 3(a)). Additionally, the observed peaks at 168.10 and 169.06 eV are presented for the S 2P3/2 and S 2P1/2, corresponding to sulfur atoms in PSS [45–47]. Notably, integrating carbon-based materials into the PEDOT:PSS matrix leads to a shift in the S 2p XPS spectra towards lower binding energies in samples pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05 as compared to pH [29,30], along with a visible change in the PEDOT:PSS ratio from 1:1.95 in pH to 1:1.67, 1:1.88, and 1:1.88 in pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05, respectively (Fig. 3(b)). Consequently, PEDOT chains reorient more easily within the matrix, fostering stronger π-π interactions between themselves and with GO and/or CQD particles, which results in higher electrical conductivity for pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05 compared to pH [29]. These findings align with the thin film resistance observations presented in Fig. S4.

The morphological characteristics of SC electrodes, particularly the elemental distributions, were examined using energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) elemental mapping images for pH, pH-GO1, pH-G0.05, and pH-R0.05 thin films (Fig. 4 ). Utilizing a spray-coating approach, all films displayed open pore structures with pronounced porosity and surface roughness, including pinholes and lumps. These morphological features engender a higher number of interfaces between active sites of SC electrodes and electrolytes, thereby augmenting storage capacitance. EDS mapping further probed the structural composition of the thin films, revealing a uniform distribution of C, O, S, and N elements across the electrodes, indicating homogeneous dispersion of GO and CQDs within the PEDOT:PSS matrix.

Secondly, SCs incorporating G-CQDs (pH-G0.05) and R-CQDs (pH-R0.05) as active electrode materials demonstrated superior volumetric capacitance compared to pristine PEDOT:PSS (pH) and commercial GO (pH-GO1) (Fig. 5(a–c)). Voltammetry curves, recorded at all scan rates, revealed that pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 exhibited the largest integral areas and current responses, signifying the highest volumetric capacitance performance (Figs. 5(a–c) and S5). This finding is consistent with the ideal triangular-shaped GCD profiles. Under ambient conditions, pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 exhibited extended charge/discharge duration times compared to both pH and pH-GO1 across all specific currents (Figs. 5(b) and S6). The calculated volumetric capacitance values at a specific current of 0.14 A cm–3 for pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 were 104.10 and 98.22 F cm−3, respectively, surpassing those of pH (56.38 F cm−3) and pH-GO1 (67.52 F cm−3) (Fig. 5(c)). Additionally, the high electrochemical performance of pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 is illustrated in the Ragone plot (Fig. 5(d)), where volumetric energy densities (in Wh cm–3) were plotted against volumetric power densities (in W cm–3). At specific currents up to 0.14 A cm–3, pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 reached peak specific energies in the range of 18.62 and 19.68 Wh cm–3 at a specific power of 0.053 W cm–3, markedly exceeding the energy densities of pH and pH-GO1 (10.61 and 12.75 Wh cm–3) at equivalent power densities, respectively.

Furthermore, Nyquist plots with the corresponding equivalent circuit, obtained at frequencies from 106 to 10–2 Hz with a 5 mV AC perturbation amplitude, are presented in Fig. 5(e). Nyquist plots for all samples consistently exhibited a straight line in the lower frequency region and a small semicircle at high frequencies [50,51]. In comparison to pH and pH-GO1, both pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 displayed smaller semicircles with reduced Warburg angles, indicating enhanced charge transfer properties and capacitance performance [2,52]. The superior electrochemical performance of pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 is further reflected in better electronic and ionic conduction, with lower equivalent series resistances of 20.33–22.99 Ω, compared to those of pH (35.13 Ω) and pH-GO1 (29.27 Ω) [53].

The charge-discharge cycling stability of SC devices is of paramount importance for their practical applications. In Figs. 5(f, b) and S7, the long-term cycling stability is illustrated over 500 consecutive charge/discharge GCD cycles at a constant current density of 2.78 A cm–3. Despite the demanding conditions of super-fast charge/discharge within a 0–1.0 V voltage range, both pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05 demonstrated remarkable cycling stability, maintaining virtually 100 % volumetric capacitance retention. This performance surpasses that of pH (89.25 %) and pH-GO1 (94.37 %). Moreover, the exceptional cycle life of pH-G0.05 was further probed during 15,000 repeated charge/discharge GCD cycles under identical conditions (Figs. 6 (a) and S8). The retention rate of pH-G0.05 achieved ∼85 % of its initial volumetric capacitance, illustrating the establishment of stable electrode/electrolyte interfaces, and affirming its aptness for high-current, rapid-charging/discharging applications. In the PEDOT:PSS/CQDs system, PEDOT predominantly enhances pseudocapacitance through faradaic processes, significantly impacting the overall capacitance of the device and thereby improving SC performance [2]. Concurrently, CQDs facilitate the electrochemical double-layer capacitance at the electrode surface, promoting faster kinetics at the electrode/electrolyte interface. This contribution results in enhanced cycling stability and rate capability, further optimizing SC device performance [2]. Crucially, our SCs employing pH doped with CQDs as the active electrode materials, demonstrate not merely an improved volumetric energy density over commercial GO but also present values that meet or surpass those documented in other studies on conductive polymer-derived SC electrodes (Fig. 6(b)).

The electrochemical characteristics of a flexible SC device incorporating pH-G0.05 electrodes were examined at different bending angles and through multiple cycles, to evaluate its adaptability and dependability for real-world use, utilizing stainless-steel foils as a substitute for rigid ITO glass (Fig. 7 ). Fig. 7(a) illustrates that the device is capable of bending at various angles, ranging from 0 to 180°. The outstanding flexibility of the SC device was established through its minimal capacitance decay under dynamic bending angles of 60, 90, 120, and 180° (Fig. 7(b–d)). The SC device maintained approximately 85 % of its initial capacitance after 3000 bending cycles at a 60° angle, showcasing its exceptional mechanical flexibility and robustness compared to other SCs reported in the literature (Table S1). Fig. 7(e–g) and Video S1 further indicate that SC devices, when integrated with pH-G0.05 electrodes and characterized by their flexibility, stand out as a viable option for portable and wearable energy storage solutions, thanks to their distinguished mechanical durability alongside exceptional cycling stability.

In summary, we have adeptly engineered high-performance SCs with electrodes composed of PEDOT:PSS and augmented by G- and R-CQDs as secondary dopants, synthesized via straightforward and rapid microwave irradiation techniques. Our study extensively investigated the effects of CQD concentrations on the electrical conductivity of thin films, unveiling their profound influence on the electrochemical capabilities of SC devices. Symmetrical double-layer, all -solid-state SC devices, crafted with the optimal concentration of CQDs (pH-G0.05 and pH-R0.05), demonstrated exceptional specific capacitances of 104.10 and 98.22 F cm−3, respectfully, surpassing those of commercial GO (pH-GO1, 67.52 F cm−3). The pH-G0.05 electrode exhibited remarkable cyclic durability, with a mere ∼15 % decrease in volumetric capacitance after 15,000 charge/discharge GCD cycles. Impressively, the pH-G0.05 composite also showcased volumetric energy densities (19.68 Wh cm−3) comparable to previous reports on conductive polymer-based SCs. Moreover, the flexible SC highlighted excellent durability and longevity, retaining over 85 % of its capacity after 3000 bending cycles. Collectively, our approach, which intertwines PEDOT:PSS with CQDs in the electrode composition, combined with scalable fabrication processes, promises significant advancements in large-area flexible SCs with superior performance and enduring cyclic stability. We believe that further investigation into alternative precursor materials to optimize CQD properties, exploration of integrating PEDOT:PSS/CQDs composites with other advanced materials, and tailoring the design and electrode composition can significantly advance this promising technology for specific applications, such as wearable electronics, energy storage systems, and hybrid energy devices.
The Supporting Information is available free of charge on the Elsevier website.

Comparative analysis of heterostructure electrode materials for SCs (Table S1); Fabrication processes are depicted in (Fig. S1); HR-TEM images (Fig. S2); thin film thickness measurements are presented in (Fig. S3); surface resistance data can be found in (Fig. S4); CV curves are detailed in (Fig. S5); GCD profiles are illustrated in (Fig. S6); GCD profiles post 500 cycles are shown in (Fig. S7), EIS plots (Fig. S8), as referenced in the main body of the text.

Bo-Seok Kim: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Sejung Kim: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Soo-Hyoung Lee: Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.
